chapters.title: A clearer route to business growth
chapters.text:
Business owners often want two things: more time and money. With so much going on today, juggling work, family, and a constantly changing world, finding enough time for everything feels hard. So, discovering ways to grow a business without wasting time is essential.

Listen to employee feedback — one pivotal idea can transform your business.

The Ask Formula is one of the strategies that can help. This method has made over $100 million in online sales, revolutionizing diverse sectors from business financing to dog training. Beyond boosting numbers, it turns indifferent customers into passionate brand advocates, ensuring repeat purchases.

Ryan Levesque, who developed the method, took a unique path to discovering it. Every successful entrepreneur faces challenges, and Ryan is no exception. His story reminds us to persist when times are hard.

The Ask Formula is a tool every company needs. This summary guides businesses, big or small, through integrating the method. What makes it unique is transparency. While some guard their "secret sauce," Ryan shares his approach freely so everyone can learn to:

• Listen to customers.
• Make more money.
• Cultivate happy, returning customers.
• Build a thriving business.

Sometimes great things are created from the most unexpected and unlikely places. ~ Ryan Levesque

Dive in to discover Ryan's experience and realize the power of asking the right questions.

chapters.title: Cutting through digital noise
chapters.text:
Today, ads are everywhere. Have you ever thought about how many advertisements you get each day? Research shows that in the 1970s, people saw around 500 ads daily. By 2006, this number had jumped to 5,000. While the exact numbers may differ now, one thing is clear: we're receiving more marketing messages than ever. So, it's natural for us to ignore most.

Many marketers increase their ads to reach potential customers. But what if there's a better way? What if companies talked to clients like friends instead of bombarding them with information? It might sound odd in a world where everything's getting bigger and louder, but genuine conversations can be more effective.

Imagine friends deciding where to dine. "Where do you want to eat?" can lead to indecision. Change it to "Would you prefer Italian or Mexican tonight?" and you get a clear answer.

Ask customers directly to meet their needs.

When Ryan first proposed this idea, it was a game-changer. He failed several times in early online marketing attempts. Desperate for a solution, he had a simple thought: why not directly ask potential customers about their reasons for purchases? That understanding led to an Ask Formula that has helped entrepreneurs in 23 different markets.

Benefits of applying this method include:

• Insightful feedback: engaging customers provides crucial understanding of preferences. Knowing what they want lets you customize offerings and build relationships that encourage returns.
• Understanding objections: when customers do not buy, feedback reveals why, allowing you to fine-tune strategies, improve products, or adapt campaigns.
• Scalability: once perfected, the method lets businesses grow without personal interaction with every customer.

While the digital world keeps changing, one thing stays the same: businesses must understand customer needs by asking, listening, and offering something helpful.

chapters.title: Dreams, setbacks, and resilience
chapters.text:
Despite his corporate and academic background in neuroscience, Ryan always wanted to be an entrepreneur. Even as a teenager, he invented and innovated, dreaming of making a significant impact. It was a way to interact with the business world and make strategic decisions. Having received a $5,000 inheritance, he invested it in the stock market instead of spending it on entertainment. Growing up without a lot of money taught him the value of every dollar. Hard work and smart financial choices were major lessons his parents taught him.

His path to success was challenging. After graduating from Brown University, he dreamed of a job combining finance and Chinese language. He accepted an offer that paid less but promised to send him to China in two years.

Moving plans were put on hold when his new boss resigned for health reasons. When he finally went to China, dreams seemed to come true — yet he realized he no longer wanted to work for someone else. Ryan was ready to start his own business.

Sometimes starting over from scratch is the best thing to do.

That realization began his path to the Ask Formula, filled with hard work, mistakes, and victories.

Ryan also navigated personal life. In 2012, becoming a dad took a toll on well-being. He loved his son but had to juggle a new business and fatherhood. Late nights, poor eating, and constant stress became normal until a health check-up forced meaningful lifestyle changes.

He understood how vital health is to chasing dreams and began prioritizing self-care, self-reflection, and unexpected growth. Struggles reminded him every victory has a cost and highlighted resilience and the ability to bounce back.

But whatever you decide, the important thing is to start. ~ Ryan Levesque

chapters.title: Online surveys as a structured start
chapters.text:
Embarking on a new methodology can be both exhilarating and daunting. But, a structured and open approach can make the process easier.

When exploring the Ask Formula, start with the following steps:

1. Quick overview: First, look at the content to get an overall understanding. This initial pass is about more than mastering the details; it's about getting a feel for the method.
2. Reread with specificity: Now, go through the content again, but with your business in mind. Consider how each part of the formula relates to your situation.
3. Put into practice: When you understand the formula well, it's time to implement it in your company. Find areas where the approach can be helpful, try it out, and then adjust based on feedback and outcomes.

Break down big tasks to make them more manageable.

So, let's take a closer look at the method Ryan invented. At the heart of the Ask Formula is the Survey Funnel Strategy. Think of it as a building plan. If the formula is a big idea guiding everything, this funnel is your hands-on manual for implementing that concept in your business.

Nowadays, businesses use online paths, or "sales funnels," to turn a person's clicks into a purchase. Imagine this: someone sees an ad online and taps on it. They land on a page asking for their name and email, offering a freebie. Then they see a sales page, decide to buy, and get a "thank you" note. That's the usual way it goes.

However, the Survey Funnel Strategy is a more innovative way to do this. It includes four main pillars:

1. The "Deep Dive" collects thoughts and feedback and reveals everyday language customers use.
2. The "Micro-Commitment Bucket" uses non-invasive questions to ease participants in and categorize them for tailored marketing.
3. The "Do You Hate Me" message goes to non-buyers by email to learn why they have not purchased.
4. The "Pivot" reaches those who have not purchased after emails, surveys, and marketing, showing you tried to offer value and would love feedback.

Let's explore each stage in detail.

chapters.title: Discover what the market actually wants
chapters.text:
Every new business or project needs to know who they're targeting. Many companies believe they understand their customers, especially if they've been in the game for some time. But relying on old ideas can backfire. Providing the Deep Dive Survey can reveal surprising insights.

A sports instructional company thought most customers were around 55. A deep survey showed the most active customers were closer to 64. They tailored ads to a new demographic and made messages feel more "60s-like," which clients loved.

Use empathy to grasp customer needs and pain points.

The secret is simple: ask them. That shapes ads and offers so they are more likely to buy.

With a list of past customers, start there. If not:

• Use someone else's email list — collaborate with friends or rent from another business.
• Promote the survey with paid traffic to a dedicated landing page inviting participation.

After groundwork before prospects enter the sales process, consider the Survey Funnel from buyers' perspective, starting with the landing page — a crucial touchpoint to get people to take the next step.

Effective landing pages include:

• Clarity: visitors instantly understand purpose and their role.
• Engagement: attractive content, often video or interactive elements, piques interest. A video explaining survey benefits can work.
• Relevance: content resonates with the audience, addressing needs, challenges, or desires so the survey feels logical.
• Call to Action: a button, link, or interactive element guiding visitors toward the survey.

Did you know? According to WordStream, global digital advertising and marketing will reach $786.2 billion by 2026, up from $350 billion in 2020.

chapters.title: Engaging prospects online with precision
chapters.text:
Internet businesses need to make real connections with potential customers. One great way to do this is by using the Micro-Commitment Bucket Survey. This survey segments your audience, ensuring each individual receives content based on their needs and tastes.

This tool has two main parts:

• Micro-Commitment: the survey starts with easy multiple-choice questions to engage clients, beginning with general queries about interests or challenges instead of personal details.
• Bucketing: respondents are segmented into categories based on answers, representing various objections or desires. Businesses can offer tailored solutions to each group.

Let customers know their voices shape your strategy.

Do not rush into this stage. First complete the Deep Dive to understand what people seek so the main survey connects well.

The next step is the Post-Survey Sales Prescription. After survey questions and contact details, visitors go to the Post-Survey "Same Visit Sale" Page serving two purposes:

• Education: offers insights based on survey responses, explaining specific concerns like a doctor explaining symptoms.
• Transition to an offer: after education, the page introduces a targeted paid solution based on feedback. Someone struggling with time management might see scheduling guidance followed by a paid course or tool.

The secret lies in personal touch — real value first, then solutions that fit what clients seek. The aim is to seal the deal on the first visit, yet deeper goals include lasting connection and trust beyond a single transaction.

chapters.title: Turning buyers into loyal customers
chapters.text:
Turning a potential client into a reliable one has numerous touchpoints. One of the most significant is the Post-Survey Sales Prescription. But the real magic happens in the Profit Maximization Upsell Sequence after this.

Imagine a customer who just purchased. They feel good and remain in a buying mindset — where upsell strategy shines. Once customers buy, they are more inclined to make additional purchases, especially when offers are relevant.

Use limited-time offers to encourage quick decisions and purchases.

At a fast-food place you might buy fries with a burger; online, a course buyer might want a planner or coaching.

Three proven upsell strategies:

• Selling value: offer more of what they bought at a better price — three more units at a discount, for example.
• Streamlining speed and ease: upsell a faster, simpler solution, such as premium software with extra features and support.
• Enhancing the initial purchase: make the first product more effective — a phone buyer might get a case or headphones.

Typically only 1–10 of 100 visitors purchase on the first visit. The Feedback Loop nurtures the rest with informative emails that provide value and answer questions. If many customers share a concern, address it in email. The final message presents options, showing the business wants to help even when the first product was not perfect.

The goal is not a one-time sale but building trust and converting visitors into loyal, recurring consumers.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
With so many online sales offers, it's tough for businesses to truly connect with customers. Think about how many more ads we see now compared to the 1970s! Despite all this noise, a simple idea stands out: companies should talk to people like friends.

Many businesses think the louder they are, the more customers will hear them. But is there a more practical approach than simply being noisy? The transformative "Ask Formula" suggests businesses can achieve more by directly engaging potential customers. Rather than making presumptions or generalizing, companies can gain invaluable insights into customer preferences, objections, and needs through research and analysis.

Online surveys play a significant role here. They allow businesses to gather feedback and group their customers based on their needs.

Remembering that the interaction shouldn't stop after a first purchase is essential. Offer your clients another product when they buy something on your website. It can add more value to their order. And businesses can stay connected through helpful emails if someone doesn't buy immediately. Continuous engagement can nurture the relationship, slowly building trust and possibly resulting in future sales.

At its core, the aim of this revolutionary marketing approach is more than a one-time deal; it's about fostering trust and loyalty.

Try this



• Invest in SEO: Optimize your online content to rank higher in search engine results.
• Leverage Referrals: Encourage satisfied customers to refer others with incentives or loyalty programs.
• Engage on Social Media: Interact with potential customers, answer queries, and post valuable content.
• Develop a Loyalty Program: Reward repeat business and encourage continued engagement.

Read it till the end?
